title: Sally

jokes.text: Sarah Fitzpatrick, Michelle Fitzpatrick, Claire Fitzpatrick... the list goes on.
jokes.setup: Jemaine brags about getting with 'lots of chicks' back in New Zealand, implying an impressive roster.
jokes.punchline: The 'list' is three women, all with the same surname — suggesting it might literally be the same family, and definitely not 'lots.'
jokes.explanation: The shared surname is the core joke — not just that his list is short, but that they're all Fitzpatricks, implying incestuous social circles or one family. The 'the list goes on' setup perfectly primes the deflation. Economical and character-specific to Jemaine's oblivious bravado.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Sarah Fitzpatrick, uh, Michelle Fitzpatrick, Claire Fitzpatrick... the list goes on.

jokes.text: That was all of them? Well, triple figures. No, that's not triple figures, that's three.
jokes.setup: Jemaine claims 'triple figures' to describe his sexual history.
jokes.punchline: Bret flatly points out that three names were listed — three is not triple figures.
jokes.explanation: The deadpan correction is perfectly executed. Bret's flat factual rebuttal is the ideal straight-man beat. Works as immediate callback to joke 1, doubling the deflation. Economy is the strength — 'that's three' is all it needs.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I don't seem to get with any women, I just talk about getting with women.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, but the ones you talk about are hot.

jokes.text: Here though, I don't seem to get with any women. I just talk about getting with women. Yeah, but the ones you talk about are hot.
jokes.setup: Jemaine laments that in NYC he only talks about women instead of getting with them.
jokes.punchline: Bret offers the consolation that the women Jemaine talks about are hotter — treating the fantasy as a legitimate upgrade.
jokes.explanation: The logic is wonderfully skewed — Bret earnestly compares imaginary women favorably to real ones. It's a sweet, dumb piece of friendship logic that tells you everything about their dynamic in a single exchange. The follow-up 'you do talk about getting with some pretty hot women' extending the absurd reasoning earns an extra beat.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: We didn't need a map. We just live down there.

jokes.text: You don't just talk about it, man, you talk about it a lot.
jokes.setup: Jemaine has accepted Bret's consolation about talking about hot women.
jokes.punchline: Bret clarifies that the talking itself is prolific — framing the volume of fantasy bragging as its own notable achievement.
jokes.explanation: A smaller extension beat on the prior joke. Functional — deepens the absurd logic without adding much new. Bret's sincerity keeps it warm rather than cruel.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I don't want to be a dick but could you guys, like, move away? I'm trying to look lonely.

jokes.text: Oh, right. We didn't need a map. We just live down there.
jokes.setup: They've been consulting a map to get to Dave's party, treating it as a navigation challenge.
jokes.punchline: They arrive and realize Dave lives near them — the elaborate navigation was pointless.
jokes.explanation: A mild observational beat establishing their slightly hapless nature. Not a strong joke but adds texture efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Chicks are attracted to lonely guys. It's a psychological analogy.

jokes.text: I don't want to be a dick, but could you guys move away? I'm trying to look lonely.
jokes.setup: Dave greets the guys at his own party but immediately seems uncomfortable with their presence.
jokes.punchline: He reveals he's deliberately performing loneliness as a seduction strategy — a preposterous social calculation delivered with total sincerity.
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely excellent joke. The social logic of performing loneliness to attract women is absurd but has a kernel of real pickup-artist reasoning, which makes it funnier. 'I don't want to be a dick' adds the perfect layer of self-aware politeness before a completely bizarre request. Dave is immediately established as an eccentric character through one line. Qualifies for 8+ because the concept is original, structure is clean, and it's entirely specific to this character.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You look good, but you look like you're waiting.

jokes.text: Chicks are attracted to lonely guys. It's a psychological analogy.
jokes.setup: Dave explains his loneliness strategy.
jokes.punchline: 'Psychological analogy' is used instead of 'psychological theory' or 'psychological phenomenon' — either Dave doesn't know what an analogy is, or he's coined a new genre of reasoning.
jokes.explanation: The malapropism 'psychological analogy' is a beautifully specific character detail. It sounds educated without being correct — Dave is the kind of person who deploys the wrong word with total confidence. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Have you tried the gulab jamuns? They're off the hook.

jokes.text: And what about Eugene? He knows what he's doing.
jokes.setup: Dave claims his loneliness strategy is working, pointing to Eugene as evidence.
jokes.punchline: The visual implication is that Eugene looks equally or more pathetically alone, or is doing something equally weird — held up as a master strategist.
jokes.explanation: Primarily a visual joke whose impact depends on what Eugene looks like. The deadpan endorsement of Eugene as a role model extends the absurdity. Rewatch bonus because the visual likely makes this land harder than the text alone.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: After six or seven weeks girls find me boring. But I'm not sure what happens, because I mean that's about how long it takes to get to know someone.

jokes.text: You look good, but you look like you're waiting.
jokes.setup: Dave is performing deliberate loneliness at his own party.
jokes.punchline: Bret's assessment — 'you look good, but you look like you're waiting' — is both a compliment and a failure notice. The loneliness strategy reads as merely... standing around.
jokes.explanation: The careful 'you look good, but...' structure is perfectly Bret — gentle, earnest, and accidentally devastating. The observation that mysterious loneliness just looks like waiting is both true and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Can you move away, please?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Sure. How long for?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: 30 minutes.

jokes.text: Have you tried the gulab jamuns? They're off the hook.
jokes.setup: Dave has just negotiated to have Bret and Jemaine stand away from him for 30 minutes to perfect his lonely image.
jokes.punchline: He immediately pivots to food recommendations — the absurd social strategist becomes a gracious host in the same breath.
jokes.explanation: 'Off the hook' applied to gulab jamuns is a mild tonal incongruity, but the real joke is the whiplash from elaborate romantic strategy to party-host hospitality. Character-specific to Dave's eccentric sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Looking 'round the room, I can tell that you are the most beautiful girl in the... room

jokes.text: After six or seven weeks girls find me boring. But I'm not sure what happens, because I mean that's about how long it takes to get to know someone.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is lamenting his romantic failures.
jokes.punchline: The punchline is implicit: he's boring once you actually know him, but he frames it as a mysterious timing coincidence rather than the obvious conclusion.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed piece of character comedy. Jemaine sees the pattern but refuses to see himself in it. The logic is airtight — 'that's about how long it takes to get to know someone' is the devastating self-own delivered as innocent observation. High character integration because it defines Jemaine's lack of self-awareness perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: In the whole wide room

jokes.text: SONG: 'Most Beautiful Girl (In the Room)' — opening verse: 'I can tell that you / Are the most beautiful girl in the... room / In the whole wide room'
jokes.setup: The song begins as a sweeping romantic ballad — 'you are the most beautiful girl in the...' with a soaring pause suggesting 'world.'
jokes.punchline: '...room.' The geographic scope of the compliment is immediately and brutally limited to the room they're in.
jokes.explanation: Among the best joke structures in the episode. The song format primes the audience for romantic grandiosity — the musical swell, the dramatic pause before the rhyme — and then delivers the smallest possible compliment. 'In the whole wide room' as a follow-up line perfectly doubles down without over-explaining. The gap between the form (earnest love ballad) and the content (deeply qualified compliment) is the engine of the whole song and it's introduced with perfect economy. Scores 9+ on structure and economy because the setup/subversion is textbook perfect and achieves maximum funny per syllable.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: And when you're on the street, depending on the street, I bet you are definitely in the top three good-looking girls on the street

jokes.text: And when you're on the street / Depending on the street / I bet you are definitely / In the top three / Good-looking girls on the street / Depending on the street
jokes.setup: Having established she's the most beautiful in the room, the song expands to streets.
jokes.punchline: 'Depending on the street' — the conditional qualifier applied twice reveals that the compliment is entirely relative to context. She might not fare as well on a nicer street.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'room' to 'street' with the added qualifier 'depending on the street' is perfect comedic construction. The repetition of the qualifier lands harder the second time. 'Top three' instead of 'number one' is another deft micro-joke. This is the song's first escalation beat and it sustains the core joke brilliantly while building.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Depending on the street

jokes.text: What is she doing / At my mate's place? / How did Dave get a hottie like that / To a party like his? / Good one, Dave / Ooh, you're a legend, Dave
jokes.setup: Standard romantic-song convention of marveling at the beloved's presence.
jokes.punchline: The song detours into incredulity that Dave — whose party we've just seen is not impressive — managed to attract an attractive woman. The compliment to the woman becomes a roast of Dave.
jokes.explanation: The song pivoting to congratulate Dave mid-romantic-ballad is a brilliant structural joke. 'To a party like his' lands because we've established the party is underwhelming. 'You're a legend, Dave' is quotably absurd. Good use of the established world to generate a song joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What is she doing at my mate's place? How did Dave get a hottie like that to a party like his?

jokes.text: I ask Dave if he's gonna make a move on you / He's not sure / I said 'Dave do you mind if I do?' / He says he doesn't mind / But I can tell he kind of minds / But I'm gonna do it anyway
jokes.setup: Romantic song convention of pursuing a woman at a party.
jokes.punchline: The narrator acknowledges Dave's discomfort but decides to proceed regardless — the romantic hero of the song is casually sociopathic about his friend's feelings, and narrates this self-awareness without shame.
jokes.explanation: This lyric does something sophisticated: it's both a character joke (Jemaine's blithe self-interest) and a structural subversion (love songs don't usually include negotiating friend permissions mid-verse). 'I can tell he kind of minds / But I'm gonna do it anyway' has the rhythm and confidence of a romantic declaration while being its moral opposite. Foreshadows the actual plot tension of the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Good one, Dave. Ooh, you're a legend, Dave

jokes.text: You're so beautiful / You could be a waitress
jokes.setup: Soaring romantic chorus declaring her beauty.
jokes.punchline: The comparison is 'waitress' — a compliment so modest it's almost an insult.
jokes.explanation: Four words and maximum impact. The musical structure builds expectation of a grand simile ('you could be a princess,' 'you could be an angel') — 'waitress' is the perfect subversion. Specific enough to be absurd, grounded enough to be plausible as something Jemaine would sincerely think is a compliment. One of the episode's best individual joke moments.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He says he doesn't mind, but I can tell he kind of minds, but I'm gonna do it anyway

jokes.text: You could be an air hostess in the '60s
jokes.setup: The 'waitress' comparison has already subverted expectations.
jokes.punchline: Air hostess escalates the aspiration slightly but adds the specific temporal qualifier 'in the '60s' — she could be attractive by the standards of a specific historical era. The precision of the caveat is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The temporal qualifier is the key detail. '60s air hostesses had a specific beauty standard — Jemaine is complimenting her relative to a historical benchmark, which is both oddly specific and gently damning. The escalation from 'waitress' to 'air hostess in the '60s' follows perfect comedic logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You could be a waitress

jokes.text: You could be a part-time model
jokes.setup: The series of modest career comparisons has been building.
jokes.punchline: 'Part-time' model — not a model, a part-time model. The qualifier again doing all the work.
jokes.explanation: By now the pattern is clear and the audience is ahead of the song — but 'part-time model' still lands because 'part-time' is the exactly right word. Not even full-time. This line becomes the song's catchphrase and is the most quotable of the career comparisons.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You could be an air hostess in the '60s

jokes.text: And then I seal the deal / I do my moves, I do my dance moves [dancing visual]
jokes.setup: The romantic song reaches its climax — the hero makes his move.
jokes.punchline: The move is 'my dance moves' — the visual of Jemaine's actual dancing (implied to be deeply uncool given the direction given later in the music video scene) undercuts any romantic credibility.
jokes.explanation: Primarily a visual/performance joke. The rewatch bonus is high because the actual choreography is where the laugh lives. Text-only it's moderate, but in context the earnest confidence in bad dancing is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You could be a part-time model

jokes.text: It's 12:02 / Just me and you / And seven other dudes / Around you on the dance floor
jokes.setup: The romantic 'just me and you' moment — classic love song intimacy.
jokes.punchline: 'And seven other dudes around you on the dance floor' — the intimate moment includes seven other men, specificity of 'seven' making it funnier than a vague 'many.'
jokes.explanation: The specific number 'seven' is the joke's best detail — not 'some' or 'many' but exactly seven dudes. The rhyme scheme setup ('just me and you') makes the intrusion of seven rivals funnier. Good structural use of the romantic song form against itself.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It's 12:02, just me and you, and seven other dudes around you on the dance floor

jokes.text: Let's get in a cab / I'll buy you a kebab / And I can't believe / That I'm sharing a kebab with the most beautiful girl / I have ever seen with a kebab
jokes.setup: The romantic escalation toward intimacy — he's going to take her somewhere special.
jokes.punchline: The destination is a kebab shop. The romantic peak is sharing a kebab. 'The most beautiful girl I have ever seen with a kebab' — the qualifying clause 'with a kebab' is the finishing touch, suggesting she's only the most beautiful in the specific context of kebab-holding.
jokes.explanation: The kebab as romantic climax is a perfect encapsulation of these characters' modest ambitions. 'The most beautiful girl I have ever seen with a kebab' is a masterpiece of subordinate clause comedy — the 'with a kebab' isn't just modifying the girl, it's qualifying the superlative itself. Highly original within the song's internal logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Let's get in a cab, I'll buy you a kebab

jokes.text: Why don't we leave? / Let's go to my house / We could feel each other up on my couch / Oh no, I don't mind / Taking it slo-o-ow
jokes.setup: The romantic song moves toward the seduction portion.
jokes.punchline: 'Feel each other up on my couch' as the romantic invitation — the language is that of a teenager in the '80s, not a confident romantic lead. 'Taking it slow' follows, which given that 'feeling up' is already the destination, is relative.
jokes.explanation: The deliberate clumsiness of 'feel each other up' — a phrase no one has used sincerely since approximately 1987 — is the joke. Jemaine presents it with romantic earnestness. The 'taking it slow' irony is a nice secondary layer.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I can't believe that I'm sharing a kebab with the most beautiful girl I have ever seen with a kebab

jokes.text: You're so beautiful / Like a tree / Or a high-class prostitute
jokes.setup: The romantic simile — 'you're so beautiful, like a...'
jokes.punchline: 'A tree / Or a high-class prostitute' — two comparisons that couldn't be further apart in register. The 'high-class' qualifier on prostitute suggests Jemaine thinks this is the more flattering option.
jokes.explanation: The collision of 'a tree' (blameless, natural, mild) with 'a high-class prostitute' is tonal whiplash done perfectly. The 'high-class' modifier is crucial — it's Jemaine's attempt to make the compliment respectful. Economy is exceptional: maximum shock per syllable.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're so beautiful, like a tree, or a high-class prostitute

jokes.text: You could be a part-time model / But you probably still have to keep your normal job / A part-time model / Spending part of your time modeling / And part of your time / Next to meeee...
jokes.setup: Return to the 'part-time model' refrain.
jokes.punchline: The callback elaborates: she'd have to keep her normal job because part-time modeling doesn't pay enough. Then the final payoff — 'spending part of your time next to me' — reveals that being with Jemaine is the other part of the part-time arrangement.
jokes.explanation: The callback pays off the part-time model joke with an elaborate logical extension. 'You probably still have to keep your normal job' is devastating in its practicality — even the compliment comes with a financial reality check. The culminating 'next to me' as the punchline reframes the whole song: he's the 'part-time' portion of her life. This is sophisticated comedic songwriting.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You could be a part-time model, but you probably still have to keep your normal job

jokes.text: # My place is usually a little tidier than this. #
jokes.setup: The romantic song has concluded; they're presumably at his apartment.
jokes.punchline: He breaks from song mid-scene to offer an apologetic disclaimer about his apartment — the seductive crooner becomes a self-conscious host in one line.
jokes.explanation: This is a meta-structural joke: sung dialogue breaking the fourth wall of the song format to offer a mundane apology. The contrast between the elaborate romantic song and the prosaic concern about apartment tidiness is the entire joke. It's delivered in song, which makes it weirder and funnier. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: A part-time model, spending part of your time modeling, and part of your time next to me

jokes.text: That's just Bret. Turn the light off, Bret. / No no no. It's okay. Leave it on.
jokes.setup: Jemaine has brought a woman home; Bret enters and turns a light on.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine tries to dismiss Bret ('that's just Bret') and get rid of the light; Sally intervenes to keep the light on — her motivation ambiguous but clearly not romantic.
jokes.explanation: Primarily a setup for the awkward scene that follows. The comedy is in the situation — Bret caught in Jemaine's romantic moment, the light as a symbol of disruption. Rewatch bonus because later revelations (Sally is Bret's ex) recontextualize the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: My place is usually a little tidier than this

jokes.text: I did all my moves. I walked on the outside of her. I bought her a kebab. I paid for half the taxi.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is confused why Sally left, listing his romantic efforts.
jokes.punchline: The 'moves' amount to: correct sidewalk positioning, a kebab, and splitting the cab fare. The specificity of 'outside of her' (chivalric sidewalk etiquette) alongside 'half the taxi' (decidedly not chivalric) is the core comedy.
jokes.explanation: This is a callback that works as character comedy: Jemaine's understanding of romance is a hybrid of outdated chivalry (outside of her) and cheap practicality (half the taxi). The kebab callback lands because we've already been prepared by the song. 'Should I have paid for the whole taxi?' is the punchline — the one genuinely generous option is his only doubt.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Turn the light off, Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: No no no. It's okay. Leave it on.

jokes.text: Did you... did you walk on the outside of her? Yes. I don't know then. I'm going to sleep.
jokes.setup: Bret is asked to diagnose why Sally left.
jokes.punchline: Having verified the one piece of chivalric information he has, Bret concludes he has no further insight and goes to sleep — the investigation is over based on sidewalk positioning.
jokes.explanation: 'I don't know then' as a definitive conclusion based solely on sidewalk positioning is perfectly deadpan. Bret's immediate retreat to sleep shows the depth of his engagement. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: So on or off with the light then?

jokes.text: Yeah, I think it might also be because she and I used to go out.
jokes.setup: Jemaine has been blaming the light for Sally's departure; Bret has been suggesting it's mainly the light. Both ignore the obvious alternate explanation.
jokes.punchline: Bret casually reveals that Sally is his ex-girlfriend — treated as a minor secondary factor compared to 'the thing with the light.'
jokes.explanation: This is a major comedic reveal delivered as a casual afterthought. The structure is brilliant: Jemaine has been obsessing over the light for several exchanges, Bret has been gently agreeing, and then Bret drops the actual reason — six months of prior relationship — as a subordinate clause. The comic engine is how long both of them avoid the elephant in the room. High scores because the delayed reveal is sophisticated and the character integration is perfect (Bret's gentle, avoidant nature makes him the exact person to sit on this information).

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I walked on the outside of her. I bought her a kebab. I paid for half the taxi.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's 'cause you and her used to go out, but also because of the thing with the light.
jokes.setup: Bret has revealed the ex-girlfriend situation.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine acknowledges it but immediately reclaims co-billing for the light, refusing to let go of his original theory.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's insistence on splitting blame between the ex situation and the light is perfect character comedy. He's not stupid — he accepts the new information — but he can't fully abandon his original theory. 'But also because of the thing with the light' is the exact phrasing of someone unwilling to be entirely wrong. High character integration score.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Should I have paid for the whole taxi?

jokes.text: She's thinking, 'Oh, this is a nice situation.' But then, 'Ugh, who turned on the light?'
jokes.setup: Jemaine reconstructs Sally's thought process from her perspective.
jokes.punchline: In his reconstruction, she was enjoying the situation until the light — the ex-boyfriend present in the apartment doesn't factor into 'nice situation' at all.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's voice acting as Sally in his own head is funny, and his version of events conveniently omits Bret's presence as a factor in the 'nice situation.' His self-serving reconstruction is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, I think it might also be because she and I used to go out.

jokes.text: Yeah, but the last thing you want to see when you're hooking up is your ex in the same room. Yeah, and you also don't want to be startled by a light, do you?
jokes.setup: Bret makes the obvious and reasonable point about the ex-in-the-room problem.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine pivots to the light again, framing it as an equivalent issue — 'startled by a light' as analogous to encountering your ex.
jokes.explanation: The running argument about the light reaches peak absurdity here: Jemaine equates 'startled by a light' with 'seeing your ex.' The debate has been escalating for several beats and this is the funniest articulation of Jemaine's position.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah. It's 'cause you and her used to go out, but also because of the thing with the light.

jokes.text: May I interject? What do you think of this? [produces a lamp] It's good. I'm getting them for the building.
jokes.setup: Jemaine and Bret are having an earnest argument about a lamp.
jokes.punchline: Murray interrupts their lamp debate by producing his own lamp for evaluation — as if the conversation has been about lamp quality all along. He's getting them 'for the building' — the argument about lamps becomes about institutional lamp procurement.
jokes.explanation: Murray materializing with a lamp at exactly this moment is a perfectly timed absurdist visual joke. The lamp debate becomes literal — Murray is in the lamp market. Good use of an established comedic environment.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: But I think it's mainly because her and I used to go out... for, like, six months.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, well... yeah, it's mainly because you used to go out, but also mainly because of the whole situation with the light.

jokes.text: Okay, guys, band meeting. Bret? Jemaine? Jemaine? Well, yeah, obviously. Here? Well, you're here? / I'm here, so why do I have to say that I'm here? / It's just so I've got it all written down, you know.
jokes.setup: Murray calls a band meeting and takes attendance.
jokes.punchline: Murray is literally taking roll call for a two-person band when everyone is already in the room. Jemaine's philosophical objection ('I'm here, so if you can see me here...') is the punchline that exposes the bureaucratic absurdity of Murray's management style.
jokes.explanation: Murray's roll-call is a perfect establishment of his character: he's a bureaucrat in the wrong profession. The three-way logic debate about why you'd call someone's name when they're visibly present is great comedy of institutional dysfunction. 'Murray, present. See? Even if I do it.' is the perfect self-referential cap.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you also don't want to be startled by a light, do you?

jokes.text: So how was, uh, Dave's party? / Oh it was good. / Mmm, it wasn't that good. / You're both aware I wasn't invited?
jokes.setup: Murray asks about the party as small talk.
jokes.punchline: Murray reveals he wasn't invited, then clarifies 'You know, eh?' suggesting he knew they knew — making the party discussion a minor social cruelty they're all now awkwardly acknowledging.
jokes.explanation: A good cringe-discomfort beat. Murray's wounded dignity combined with his formality ('you're both aware I wasn't invited?') is funny. 'Jemaine knew' written down in the minutes is the deadpan escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It doesn't work.

jokes.text: 'Jemaine knew.' [Murray writes it in the minutes]
jokes.setup: Murray learns Jemaine knew he wasn't invited to Dave's party.
jokes.punchline: Murray formally records this social slight in the official band meeting minutes — bureaucratizing a personal grievance.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional economy comedy. Three words and a gesture perfectly establish Murray's character — the manager who processes emotional slights through bureaucratic procedure. The formalization of 'Jemaine knew' as meeting minutes is an inspired detail that would resonate through the whole series. Economy score is near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Murray, present. See? Even if I do it.

jokes.text: What fan base? / The fan base of the band. / You mean Mel? / That's not a fan base, that's just a woman.
jokes.setup: Murray discusses increasing the 'American fan base.'
jokes.punchline: Their entire American fan base is one person — Mel. And she's 'just a woman,' not a base.
jokes.explanation: The reveal of a one-person fan base is a great joke about their status, delivered with wonderful economy. 'That's not a fan base, that's just a woman' is highly quotable and establishes their career situation with brutal efficiency.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're both aware I wasn't invited?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Jemaine, did you know?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You did know?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You know, eh?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Thanks.

jokes.text: Yeah, but I'm calling it a fan base from now on. It's just easier when I call, because if you say, 'Oh, the fan will be there,' they can tell there's only one person. I'm trying to make it look bigger.
jokes.setup: Bret has established that the 'fan base' is one person.
jokes.punchline: Murray explains his semantic strategy: 'fan base' sounds bigger than 'fan.' His awareness that venues would notice 'the fan' vs. 'the fan base' reveals the gap between their aspirations and reality.
jokes.explanation: Murray's explanation of 'base' as a word that sounds bigger is a good character beat — he's doing genuine PR work for a band with one fan. The logic is both absurd and completely understandable.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What fan base?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: The fan base of the band.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You mean Mel?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's not a fan base, that's just a woman.

jokes.text: What happened with the gig at the Aquarium? / It was kind of a misunderstanding. / Um, there was a typo in the ad. / It was sand they wanted. / Sand, you know, that wavy font? / It looks like a 'B' but it was an 'S.' / But I sent the demo.
jokes.setup: The band lost a gig at the Aquarium and wants to know why.
jokes.punchline: A font confusion — 'Sand' font looks like a 'B' so they read 'sand' as 'band.' Murray sent the demo anyway. And it's playing in the lobby.
jokes.explanation: The Sand/Band font confusion is an inspired piece of absurdist comedy that feels grounded in real bureaucratic incompetence. Murray's 'but I sent the demo' is perfect — his one assertive action was still useless. The payoff that they 'liked it' and might play it in the lobby is the cherry — partial success at the Aquarium for a band that didn't play there.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, but I'm calling it a fan base from now on. It's just easier when I call, because if you say, 'Oh, the fan will be there,' they can tell there's only one person.

jokes.text: They liked it. They might play it in the lobby. / Okay, that's positive.
jokes.setup: Murray reveals the demo sent to the Aquarium has had an outcome.
jokes.punchline: The outcome is lobby ambient music. Bret's 'okay, that's positive' accepts this as genuine progress.
jokes.explanation: Bret's earnest 'that's positive' about their music being used as lobby ambient noise is a perfect piece of deadpan understatement. The lowness of the bar they're clearing is established gently.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: There was a typo in the ad. It was sand they wanted. Sand, you know, that wavy font? It looks like a 'B,' but it was an 'S.'

jokes.text: Item two... that's it.
jokes.setup: Murray announces 'item two' of the band meeting agenda.
jokes.punchline: Item two does not exist. The agenda ends at one item — the meeting is essentially over.
jokes.explanation: The pause between 'Item two' and 'that's it' is perfect timing comedy. Murray's formality of announcing item numbers for a meeting that has nothing to discuss is a great character beat. Economy is essentially perfect — three words do the work.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But I sent the demo.
So the good news is that, uh... they liked it. They might play it in the lobby.

jokes.text: How does Saturday morning sound?
jokes.setup: Murray was trying to get Friday afternoon off for the music video.
jokes.punchline: After a phone call, he comes back with Saturday morning — presumably he couldn't get Friday. The music video will be shot on Saturday morning, the least glamorous possible time slot.
jokes.explanation: The callback payoff from Murray's phone call — he couldn't even get Friday afternoon, so music video day is Saturday morning. 'How does Saturday morning sound?' delivered as if offering a genuine upgrade is perfect Murray character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Oh, hey guys.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hey, Mel.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Hey, Mel.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Hey, whoa. Crazy meeting you here, huh?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What, outside our house?

jokes.text: Hey, whoa. Crazy meeting you here, huh? / What, outside our house?
jokes.setup: Mel 'runs into' Bret and Jemaine outside their apartment building.
jokes.punchline: Bret's flat response — 'what, outside our house?' — establishes that Mel is loitering outside their home and pretending it's a coincidence.
jokes.explanation: Bret's 'what, outside our house?' is perfectly deadpan — he doesn't accuse her of stalking, he just notes the absurdity of calling it a coincidence in this location. Efficiently establishes Mel's obsessive-fan quality.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: I went to your gig. I was at the Aquarium for hours just waiting. They wouldn't let me stay after 1:00 a.m.

jokes.text: Jogging. Hey, where were you guys on Tuesday? I went to your gig. I was at the Aquarium for hours just waiting. They wouldn't let me stay after 1:00 a.m.
jokes.setup: Mel explains why she was outside their house (jogging) and pivots to attending the canceled Aquarium gig.
jokes.punchline: She waited at the Aquarium until 1:00 a.m. for a gig that was canceled — dedication bordering on obsession. 'They wouldn't let me stay after 1:00 a.m.' implies she argued for the right to stay.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Aquarium misunderstanding pays off well — Mel's presence at 1:00 a.m. is both funny as a fan dedication joke and as a character establishment moment. Her tone treats this as completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Well, the fish were beautiful. They were breeding... it's mating season and I... I saw fish make love.

jokes.text: The fish were beautiful. They were breeding — it's mating season and I... I saw fish make love.
jokes.setup: Mel is consoling herself about the wasted trip.
jokes.punchline: 'I saw fish make love' — the upgrade from standard animal observation to romantic framing of fish reproduction, delivered with sincere wonder.
jokes.explanation: 'Fish make love' is a beautifully odd turn of phrase. Mel's genuine enjoyment of fish reproduction as a consolation prize is perfectly in character — slightly off-kilter sincerity that defines the show's tone.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You... you just came from home.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're just popping out for a second and then, um, that's all we were going to do... just get some air.

jokes.text: Actually, you know quite a few species of fish require two or more sexual partners...
jokes.setup: Bret shuts down the fish reproduction conversation; Mel pivots to fish sexuality as a topic.
jokes.punchline: Mel pivots to detailed fish sexual biology unprompted, treating it as a conversational escalation rather than a shutdown — and she knows specific facts about fish sexual requirements.
jokes.explanation: Mel's fish biology knowledge is an odd-angle cringe beat — she's not being deliberately inappropriate, she just happens to know niche details about fish reproduction. The boys' discomfort is the straight-man reaction.

dialogue.line: It's a good cake, man.
It doesn't make any difference... Isabella.
I don't want cake...
It's top quality.
Get out. I don't want your cake!
20 bucks. Give me the TV.

jokes.text: We always have such a fun time when we're hanging out together. Awesome. [Bret and Jemaine awkward silence]
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine have been trying to escape the conversation with Mel.
jokes.punchline: Mel characterizes their evasive retreat as a fun hangout — either she's oblivious to their discomfort or reframing it entirely. The beat depends on Bret and Jemaine's silent, politely pained response.
jokes.explanation: Primarily a reaction beat. Mel's cheerful assessment of an awkward non-interaction as a 'fun hangout' is good character comedy. The visual of Bret and Jemaine's response is where the laugh lives.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: What an idiot, trying to sell me a cake.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It was a beautiful cake.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, it was.

jokes.text: It doesn't make any difference... Isabella. / I don't want cake... / It's top quality. / Get out. I don't want your cake! / What an idiot, trying to sell me a cake.
jokes.setup: The scene cuts to Dave's shop, where a customer is selling Dave a cake.
jokes.punchline: Dave is apparently running a business where a man keeps trying to sell him cakes he doesn't want. 'What an idiot, trying to sell me a cake' is delivered with genuine bafflement, as if cake-selling has no logic.
jokes.explanation: The abruptness of the cake-selling scene is funny in itself — a pure piece of absurdist world-building. Dave's shop apparently deals in legitimate goods and is regularly accosted by cake sellers. 'What an idiot, trying to sell me a cake' as a completely deadpan observation is good character establishment.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Hey, Jemaine, I don't know how they do things back in England...

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: New Zealand.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Yeah, whatever. I don't really give a shit.

jokes.text: It was a beautiful cake. / Yeah, it was.
jokes.setup: Dave has just thrown a cake salesman out of his shop.
jokes.punchline: Both Bret and Jemaine immediately and solemnly agree the cake was beautiful — a moment of shared sincerity about cake aesthetics in the middle of a business dispute.
jokes.explanation: The shared, solemn agreement about the cake's beauty is a perfect deadpan beat. Bret and Jemaine's priorities are revealed: they can agree on the cake's merit even as Dave is furious. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, there was four of them so more of a love square, but, you know, no one gets on.

jokes.text: Hey, Jemaine, I don't know how they do things back in England... / New Zealand. / Yeah, whatever. I don't really give a shit.
jokes.setup: Dave is about to give Jemaine cultural advice.
jokes.punchline: Dave calls them English, is corrected, and immediately dismisses the correction — 'I don't really give a shit' about where they're from.
jokes.explanation: The England/New Zealand confusion played for American-indifference comedy. Dave's immediate 'I don't really give a shit' after the correction is refreshingly honest. Sets up the pattern of American characters not distinguishing New Zealand from other accents.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Mind you, they did make some of their best music back then.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: 'Rumors.'

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, that's all true.

jokes.text: You don't get with your bandmate's girlfriend, past or present. / Yes, thanks for that. / You get a love triangle. / You know? Fleetwood Mac situation. / Well, there was four of them so more of a love square, but, you know, no one gets on. / Mind you, they did make some of their best music back then. 'Rumors.' / No, that's all true.
jokes.setup: Dave warns about the love triangle danger with bandmates.
jokes.punchline: Dave corrects his own metaphor (love triangle → love square), then immediately undermines his own advice by noting Fleetwood Mac made great music precisely during that period. His cautionary tale becomes a recommendation.
jokes.explanation: The Fleetwood Mac digression is funny because Dave does the geometric correction himself and then immediately sabotages his own advice. 'Mind you, they did make some of their best music back then' is the punchline — his cautionary tale proves the opposite of what he intended. Jemaine's 'no, that's all true' is a perfect dry acknowledgment.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I don't think that's a proper camera, Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, a mobile-phone camera.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I think it's mostly a phone.

jokes.text: And... action! / I don't think that's a proper camera, Murray. / Yeah, a mobile-phone camera. / I think it's mostly a phone.
jokes.setup: Murray has offered to shoot their music video.
jokes.punchline: His 'camera' is a mobile phone — he calls 'action' with director's authority, then defends the equipment.
jokes.explanation: A functional visual gag — the grandiosity of 'action!' against a mobile phone camera is a reliable joke. 'I think it's mostly a phone' is the best line. Not exceptional but efficiently establishes the music video shoot's low budget.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Those are your function buttons for the robot.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: They look like nipples.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Don't touch them!

jokes.text: What are these supposed to be? / Those are your function buttons for the robot. / They look like nipples. / Don't touch them!
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine are in homemade robot costumes for the music video.
jokes.punchline: The 'function buttons' for the robot costume look like nipples. Murray's panicked 'don't touch them' suggests he's aware of the issue but committed to the design.
jokes.explanation: A good visual comedy setup. 'Don't touch them!' as Murray's defensive response to the nipple observation is the best line — he's aware the design is compromised but is committed to his creative vision.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It doesn't look like Daft Punk.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We wanted ones like Daft Punk.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I don't know who he is.

jokes.text: It doesn't look like Daft Punk. We wanted ones like Daft Punk. I don't know who he is.
jokes.setup: The band is unhappy their costumes don't match their Daft Punk inspiration.
jokes.punchline: Murray doesn't know who Daft Punk is. He made robot costumes without reference to the actual aesthetic they wanted.
jokes.explanation: Murray's complete ignorance of the creative reference he was supposed to be emulating is a perfect management-incompetence joke. 'I don't know who he is' — Murray doesn't even know Daft Punk is two people. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The distant future, the year 2000

jokes.text: Try not to walk so funky, Bret, because you're... try and make it less cool. More like... you know, electronic... hide your neck.
jokes.setup: Murray is directing the robot walk for the music video.
jokes.punchline: 'Try and make it less cool' is a director's note no one has ever given. 'Hide your neck' as a robotic acting note is beautifully specific and absurd.
jokes.explanation: 'Make it less cool' is a genuinely original director's note that inverts every acting note ever given. 'Hide your neck' is the specific absurdist detail that makes it great. Bret's apparent natural walking is described as 'funky' — implying the character is too cool to play a robot convincingly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We no longer say 'yes', instead we say 'affirmative'

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yes, affirmative

jokes.text: SONG: 'The Humans Are Dead' — 'It is the distant future, the year 2000 / We are robots'
jokes.setup: The robots announce they are from 'the distant future.'
jokes.punchline: The distant future is the year 2000 — already in the recent past when the show aired. The precision of '2000' as 'distant future' is either robotic miscalculation or cheerful absurdism.
jokes.explanation: 'The distant future, the year 2000' is an inspired piece of robot comedy — the temporal scope is both grandiose and anticlimactic. The robots' sense of the future is wildly miscalibrated, which is itself a joke about robot intelligence. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Unless it's a more colloquial situation with a few robo-friends

jokes.text: We no longer say 'yes' / Instead we say 'affirmative' / Yes, affirmative / Unless it's a more colloquial situation / With a few robo-friends
jokes.setup: Robots are describing their post-human world and its linguistic rules.
jokes.punchline: The robot language rule has an exception for casual situations — robots have informal speech registers with their friends. The immediate self-contradiction ('yes, affirmative') and the social nuance undermine robotic absolutism.
jokes.explanation: This is sophisticated comedy writing disguised as robot nonsense. The robots have a formal linguistic rule but immediately introduce a colloquial exception for 'robo-friends' — which means they have the same social register distinctions as humans, completely defeating the point. 'Yes, affirmative' as the example of formal speech is also a self-contradicting line.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We used poisonous gases and we poisoned their asses

jokes.text: There is only one kind of dance... the robot / And the robo-boogie / Oh yes / Two kinds of dances
jokes.setup: 'There is only one kind of dance...' suggests robotic austerity.
jokes.punchline: Immediately corrected to two dances. The robot can't even maintain its own authoritative declarations for one beat.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best constructed jokes. The setup 'there is only one kind of dance...' builds expectation for a grand singular statement, the pause and 'the robot' lands it, and then 'and the robo-boogie / oh yes / two kinds of dances' immediately collapses the robot's authority. The self-correction delivered with total sincerity — 'two kinds of dances' — is the perfect punchline. Structure is nearly flawless.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The humans are dead

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He's right they are dead

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The humans are dead

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: They look like they're dead

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It had to be done

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'll just confirm they're dead

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So that we could have fun

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Affirmative, I poked one, it was dead

jokes.text: We used poisonous gases / And we poisoned their asses
jokes.setup: The robots are explaining how they killed all humans — a grand, dramatic revelation.
jokes.punchline: 'Poisoned their asses' — the rhyme forces a crude word into an otherwise deadpan historical account. The robots chose this specific rhyme for their official record.
jokes.explanation: The gases/asses rhyme is a classic comedy technique — forcing a crude word through the pressure of rhyme scheme — but the execution is excellent because the robots deliver it with complete historical gravity. The couplet has become one of the most quoted lines from the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: So you get more than one 'one'?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Some people are lucky. I've had a few ones.

jokes.text: The humans are dead / He's right they are dead / The humans are dead / They look like they're dead / It had to be done / I'll just confirm they're dead / So that we could have fun / Affirmative, I poked one, it was dead.
jokes.setup: Robot call-and-response about the death of all humans.
jokes.punchline: The second robot's responses become progressively more bureaucratic and less decisive: 'they look like they're dead,' 'I'll just confirm they're dead,' and finally 'I poked one, it was dead' — the method of confirmation being a poke.
jokes.explanation: The escalating qualifications on 'they're dead' — from confident assertion to 'they look like they're dead' to 'I'll just confirm' to 'I poked one, it was dead' — is brilliantly constructed. The scientific rigor of poking a human to confirm death is the perfect absurdist punchline. 'Affirmative, I poked one, it was dead' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode. Earned high scores: structure and escalation are exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: So how many ones can you have?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Five.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: How many have you had?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Three.

jokes.text: It's just that I think she might be the one. / Sally? / Yeah. / What makes you think that? / You just know. When it happens to you, you'll know. / You said Michelle was the one. / Yeah, she's the one. / You said Claire was the one. / Yeah, she's another one.
jokes.setup: Jemaine declares Sally 'the one.'
jokes.punchline: Bret reveals Jemaine has previously designated multiple women 'the one' — and Jemaine doesn't retract these designations but upgrades 'the one' to a non-exclusive category.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's casual reframing of 'the one' as a plural category — 'she's another one' — is a great character beat. The logic that 'some people are lucky, I've had a few ones' completely dismantles the romantic singularity of the concept while Jemaine treats it as perfectly coherent. Good callback energy to the Fitzpatrick list.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: How's that going?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's going pretty good. I still need to get more body in the hair.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It pretty much looks like a helmet.

jokes.text: So how many ones can you have? / Five. / How many have you had? / Three. / How many have you had? / Just one. Just one.
jokes.setup: The conversation about 'the ones' has established a quantifiable system.
jokes.punchline: Bret has had just one 'one' — and his deflated 'just one. Just one.' implies this is embarrassingly few in Jemaine's inflated economy of 'ones.' Then the irony: Bret has the correct romantic relationship to the concept.
jokes.explanation: Bret's 'just one. Just one.' — the repetition and pause — implies shame at having only one 'one.' The joke inverts romantic idealism: Bret is the romantic purist by accident, Jemaine is the serial monogamist who's redefined the concept. The follow-up awkward silence is also part of the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's helmet-hair night, man, not really. I mean, I've been thinking about it for ages. It's on the board.

jokes.text: Do you think it would be okay if Sally and I had the apartment to ourselves tonight? / What for? / Some time alone. / Mmm, I was going to be working on my secret project. / Your helmet that looks like your hair?
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks Bret to leave the apartment for a date with Sally.
jokes.punchline: Bret's 'secret project' is a helmet designed to look like his hair — a project so specific and low-stakes that it's immediately identifiable as exactly that.
jokes.explanation: The helmet-that-looks-like-his-hair is one of the episode's best individual character jokes. It's perfectly specific (not a 'project' in general but this exact project), completely absurd (why would you need this?), and reveals Bret's creative priorities. Jemaine's immediate identification of it — 'your helmet that looks like your hair?' — suggests this is an established, known project in their world. The 'secret' designation for something Jemaine already knows about is the capper. Qualifies for 8.7: highly original, perfectly structured, quintessentially Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: You know, it is actually a bit weird with you here, Bret.

jokes.text: - It pretty much looks like a helmet. - Right.
jokes.setup: Bret has been working on a helmet that looks like his hair.
jokes.punchline: It doesn't look like his hair. It looks like a helmet.
jokes.explanation: A quick callback beat that confirms the project's failure. 'Right' as Bret's acknowledgment that the helmet looks like a helmet is deadpan acceptance. Functional rather than exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You do look beautiful, I was just about to say that.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: Bret, this is my date.

jokes.text: It's helmet-hair night, man, not really. I mean, I've been thinking about it for ages. It's on the board. You know about it. You said you were going to help actually.
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks Bret to swap helmet-hair night for a different night.
jokes.punchline: Bret reveals the helmet-hair project has its own scheduled night, is on the board, and Jemaine had previously agreed to assist — the logistical apparatus around this eccentric project is fully developed.
jokes.explanation: The helmet-hair project has its own calendar commitment — 'it's on the board' — and Jemaine was supposed to help because Bret can't see what he's doing when wearing it. The escalating seriousness of the logistics around a helmet-that-looks-like-hair is very funny. 'Because I can't really see what I'm doing when I've got it on' is the best line.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I wouldn't have ordered the duck. I spent all my money on the duck. Would you be able to bring the duck home?

jokes.text: How about you just maybe don't go out with Sally so I can hang out with you guys? / That'd be good.
jokes.setup: Bret is negotiating who gets the apartment.
jokes.punchline: Bret's solution to the problem is for Jemaine not to date Sally at all — stated in the mildest possible terms — and then immediately agreeing it would be good as if it's a reasonable request.
jokes.explanation: Bret's suggestion — phrased as the most passive request imaginable — reveals his actual feelings about the situation. Jemaine's reflexive 'that'd be good' is a good beat before he backtracks. The mildness is the character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: I just... sometimes I think the thing you're looking for is often standing right in front of your eyes.

jokes.text: She used to like you. She really likes me.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is listing reasons it won't be weird for Bret to join his date with Sally.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine describes Bret's past relationship with Sally as 'she used to like you' — past tense, downgraded — and his own as 'she really likes me,' present tense with emphasis. He's both reassuring Bret and subtly ranking himself above him.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between 'she used to like you' and 'she really likes me' is delivered as reassurance but functions as a status declaration. Jemaine's obliviousness to the cruelty of this distinction is perfect character work. Economy is excellent — the tense difference and the 'really' do the whole joke.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You can tell me anything. Anything. I promise I won't think you're a pervert.

jokes.text: No, it's going to be weird. / It won't be weird. / That is going to be weird. / It won't be weird. / It will be weird. / It won't be weird.
jokes.setup: Jemaine insists bringing Bret along on his date won't be weird.
jokes.punchline: The argument loops and escalates in pure repetition — neither character adds new arguments, just increasing conviction — until they're in a pure weird/won't-be-weird standoff.
jokes.explanation: The repetition loop is a classic absurdist comedy technique — argument without progress. Works well because it leads directly into the next scene where the prediction is immediately confirmed.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Have I shown you my picture of Jemaine's lips? I have it here in my wallet. Look. Those are my cousins, and there... that's... that's Jemaine's lips.

jokes.text: You know, it is actually a bit weird with you here, Bret. / Yeah, I should go. I'll go.
jokes.setup: Jemaine insisted it wouldn't be weird; they are now at the restaurant.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine immediately concedes it is weird — confirming Bret's prediction — with no apparent awareness of the irony of his immediate capitulation.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the weird/won't be weird debate is perfectly constructed — Jemaine's immediate concession at the restaurant validates everything Bret said, and Bret's 'yeah, I should go. I'll go.' is resigned acceptance without any 'I told you so.' The structure of the callback is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Whenever I was watching the two of you it was obvious that you were more into her than she was into you. Yeah, you could really tell. She always looked kind of bored.

jokes.text: Bret was saying that it was going to be weird with him here and I said it wouldn't be weird, but it is a bit weird.
jokes.setup: Jemaine addresses Sally about why Bret should leave.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine explains the history of the debate to Sally — including his own incorrect prediction — with no embarrassment, as if delivering a neutral factual account.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine narrating his own wrong prediction to Sally with complete equanimity is good character work — he has no defensiveness about having been wrong, he just states the facts. 'It is a bit weird' is the deadpan confirmation.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: You looked kind of cute when I was drunk.
Maybe what I really want is an Australian.

jokes.text: Bret, this is my date.
jokes.setup: Bret has just told Sally she looks beautiful — to his ex-girlfriend on Jemaine's date.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's flat reminder — 'Bret, this is my date' — responds to Bret's romantic overture toward Sally as if it's a simple identity confusion.
jokes.explanation: Four words that do enormous situational comedy work. Jemaine's flat delivery treats the complexity of the situation as a simple clarification issue. Maximum economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You can't break my heart, it's liquid. It melted when I met you.

jokes.text: It's just feelings.
jokes.setup: Bret has just told his ex-girlfriend on someone else's date that she looks beautiful.
jokes.punchline: 'It's just feelings' — Bret's explanation/apology for the emotional overshare is both completely inadequate and completely true.
jokes.explanation: 'It's just feelings' is an exceptional three-word punchline. It's simultaneously an apology, an explanation, a deflection, and an unintentionally deep statement about human behavior. Perfectly deadpan, perfectly Bret. Economy score near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm not crying, it's just been raining on my face

jokes.text: Actually, you know what, it's just like when Bret and I went out and you were always around. / I've ordered my main, I'll stay.
jokes.setup: Sally is suggesting Bret should leave — invoking the prior relationship dynamic where Jemaine was always present.
jokes.punchline: Bret's response to being told to leave is that he's already ordered his main course and will stay — a completely practical reason that ignores all romantic and emotional context.
jokes.explanation: 'I've ordered my main, I'll stay' is a great character punchline — Bret's emotional needs are secondary to the logistics of his meal order. The mundane practical concern is the perfect Bret response to a heightened emotional situation.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: And if I am crying, it's not because of you, it's because I'm thinking about friend of mine that you don't know who is dying

jokes.text: Um... I wouldn't have ordered the duck. / I spent all my money on the duck. / Would you be able to bring the duck home?
jokes.setup: Bret has agreed to leave the restaurant after a cringe-y scene.
jokes.punchline: He regrets ordering the duck specifically. Then reveals it cost all his money. Then asks for the duck to be brought home — his departure requires takeaway duck logistics.
jokes.explanation: The duck sequence is a brilliant piece of escalating absurdism. First: regret about the choice. Second: it was all his money. Third: logistical request for duck transportation. Each beat escalates Bret's focus on the duck over the emotional situation. Highly quotable. The mundane specificity of 'the duck' as the anchor of the scene is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I've just been cutting onions, I'm making a lasagna for one

jokes.text: That was weird, wasn't it? / Yeah, a little.
jokes.setup: Bret has left, the scene has been extremely awkward.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine characterizes the entire preceding sequence as 'a little' weird — massive understatement — and Sally's 'yeah, a little' matches his register.
jokes.explanation: A functional callback understatement. Not exceptional on its own but serves as the period at the end of the restaurant sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: For your information, there's an inflammation in my tear gland

jokes.text: I never thought she was right for you anyway. / What didn't you like about Sally? / Well, I haven't actually met Sally.
jokes.setup: Mel is consoling Bret about Sally, offering opinions on their compatibility.
jokes.punchline: She hasn't met Sally. Her strong opinions about Sally's unsuitability are entirely uninformed.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Mel is offering confident relationship advice about someone she's never met is a good character joke — Mel's opinions are generated by loyalty and obsession rather than information. Well-structured with a clean reveal.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: My eyes are just a little sweaty today, they've been looking around a lot, searching for you

jokes.text: I just sometimes think the thing you're looking for is often standing right in front of your eyes.
jokes.setup: Mel is consoling Bret about his romantic life.
jokes.punchline: Mel is delivering a romantic cliché that transparently refers to herself — except Bret, mercifully, doesn't seem to register it as such.
jokes.explanation: The line itself is a cliché, which is intentional — Mel is deploying romantic movie language in a delusional context. The comedy is in the dramatic irony (Bret doesn't notice) and Mel's complete sincerity. Character integration is good but the joke itself leans on an overused formula.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What did you cook?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Are you trying to get back together with Sally?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No. Just making chitchat.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, okay.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Macaroni.

jokes.text: Mel, can we go now? We've been here for 2½ hours. / Is that Doug? / That's Doug. Don't worry about him.
jokes.setup: Mel is consoling Bret; a male voice calls from offscreen.
jokes.punchline: Mel's husband Doug has been waiting with her for 2½ hours while she talks to the band she's obsessed with. His complaint and her dismissal reveal the full extent of her fan behavior.
jokes.explanation: Doug's 2½ hour wait outside Bret's apartment establishes the depth of Mel's fan obsession — she's dragged her husband to loiter in a stairwell for over two hours. 'Don't worry about him' as a dismissal of her own husband is perfect character comedy. Good payoff to the earlier Mel introduction.

jokes.text: Just 'cause I'm married to Doug doesn't mean I can't be here for you. / Thanks, Mel. / You can tell me anything. Anything. I promise I won't think you're a pervert.
jokes.setup: Mel is reassuring Bret she's available for emotional support.
jokes.punchline: 'I promise I won't think you're a pervert' — an unsolicited reassurance suggesting either she frequently makes this judgment about men or she's preemptively green-lighting personal conversation.
jokes.explanation: 'I promise I won't think you're a pervert' is an odd and funny piece of unsolicited reassurance. Its specificity — 'pervert' rather than any other judgment — implies this is something Mel thinks about with some regularity. Good character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Come on, sucker, lick my battery

jokes.text: Have I shown you my picture of Jemaine's lips? / No. / I have it here in my wallet. Look. / Those are my cousins, and there... that's... that's Jemaine's lips.
jokes.setup: Mel is consoling Bret, the conversation about Jemaine having just been established.
jokes.punchline: Mel has a photo of Jemaine's lips specifically — not Jemaine, just the lips — in her wallet among family photos.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode and one of the best jokes in the series. Multiple layers: (1) she has a photo of lips specifically, not the whole person; (2) it's in her wallet alongside family photos; (3) it's filed between cousins as if Jemaine's lips are a family member; (4) she's showing it to Bret during what should be emotional support for him. Every element is precisely wrong in a maximally funny way. The photo is 'just the lips' — not even Jemaine's face. This is 9+ caliber: original, structurally perfect, utterly character-specific to Mel, and deeply quotable. Comparable in quality to the anchor examples.

jokes.text: Bret, don't worry about Sally, all right? Whenever I was watching the two of you it was obvious that you were more into her than she was into you. / Really? / Yeah, you could really tell. / She always looked kind of bored.
jokes.setup: Mel is now offering more direct feedback about Bret and Sally's former relationship.
jokes.punchline: Mel was apparently watching Bret and Sally's relationship with close observation — 'whenever I was watching the two of you' implies sustained surveillance — and her 'comfort' is to tell him Sally found him boring.
jokes.explanation: 'Whenever I was watching the two of you' is the key phrase — confirming she was actively watching them as a couple. Her consolation being 'Sally looked bored' is accidentally devastating. Good character comedy that reinforces both Mel's obsessive nature and her complete inability to provide appropriate emotional support.

jokes.text: 'These aren't tears of sadness because you're leaving me / I've just been cutting onions / I'm making a lasagna / For one'
jokes.setup: Another excuse for the tears — this time, cooking
jokes.punchline: 'I'm making a lasagna / For one' — the addition of 'for one' is the brutal button: the onion excuse is undermined by the revelation that the lasagna is a solitary meal, making the excuse simultaneously about denial AND loneliness
jokes.explanation: This is arguably the episode's finest single comedic moment. 'I'm making a lasagna / For one' does more work than almost any other line in the episode. The onion excuse is a well-worn trope — but the lasagna for one transforms it: he's not just denying crying, he's painting a picture of himself alone at home making a full lasagna for himself, which is MORE sad than crying, and the 'for one' is delivered as a clincher to the denial while accidentally being the most devastating admission. The economy is extraordinary — two words ('for one') do the work of an entire emotional arc. This is a 9+ joke.

jokes.text: 'I'm not weeping because you won't be here to hold my hand / For your information / There's an inflammation in my tear gland'
jokes.setup: The song continues its catalogue of non-crying excuses
jokes.punchline: The medical diagnosis 'inflammation in my tear gland' — rhymed with 'hold my hand' — is both a ridiculous excuse and a surprisingly technical one, delivered as if medical authority makes it more credible
jokes.explanation: 'For your information, there's an inflammation in my tear gland' is a great line — the pseudo-medical terminology ('tear gland') sounds almost plausible, the 'for your information' prefix implies defensive authority, and the rhyme with 'hand' makes it sound like it was written specifically to justify itself. The escalation from natural phenomena to medical diagnosis is good comedic logic.

tmdb_overview: Bret and Jemaine go to a party at Dave's, where Jemaine meets and falls for the 'most beautiful girl in the room', Sally. When it turns out that Sally used to go out with Bret, things get weird between Bret and Jemaine.
